data.title: General Mills: Value Appeal Seems Insufficient
data.description:
The Thesis

General Mills (NYSE:GIS) may still generate modest returns above its cost of capital, but structural headwinds are eroding its core business, and a turnaround seems a long way off. Changing consumer habits, the rise of GLP-1 drugs, reducing snack consumption, and private-label competition have put pressure on domestic revenue and margins in the company's main segments.

In a way, all the appeal that GIS has built around generating value for its shareholders is undermined by very low growth prospects and an unconvincing path to turnaround toward stability and eventually minimal growth ahead.

Although by checking some value investing boxes, some investors may be attracted to the thesis after share price levels reach very depressed levels, it seems very clear to me that the market should not, and will not, reward a company that is not growing its revenues; on the contrary, it is shrinking.

I do not see any short- to medium-term scenarios that could significantly alter the outlook for a turnaround in the bearish momentum for GIS, and in this article, I will delve deeper into these points.

A Not So Generous General Mills

In the not-so-distant past, General Mills' stock was synonymous with stability, defense, and generosity in shareholder returns. Results in line with market expectations and attractive dividend yields confirmed the stock's defensive appeal. But for a long time now, it has been underappreciated by the market, for obvious reasons.

Momentum is awful. Over the last decade, investors who bought GIS saw the company's value decline by 11.8%, while the broader market jumped 233% over the same period. Shortening the time periods, GIS continued to underperform over the last 5 years, 3 years, one year, and year-to-date, six months, one month, one week... ouch.

However, it wasn't all tragedy between these periods. Between 2011 and 2016, GIS shares had appreciated ~100%, a period marked by strategic acquisitions, such as the purchase of Yoplaite, but they collapsed by about 50% by 2018 with the onset of more significant changes in consumption and competitive pressures. Then, from 2018 to May 2023, shares rebounded up to 140% again, with a refocus on key brands and expansion in the pet food sector. Until they plummeted again by 45% to the level they are at today.

The image below clearly shows the most recent reason that has been putting pressure on processed food companies. Between 2023 and mid-2024, the boom in GLP-1 weight loss drugs and a new generation that is increasingly avoiding "bad foods" in favor of a healthier lifestyle clearly drew the line between winners and losers—even though GLP-1 companies such as Novo Nordisk (NVO) and Eli Lilly (LLY) have faced recent headwinds unrelated to demand for weight loss drugs.

Data have shown that about 12% of U.S. adults have used a GLP-1 drug, and 6% have used one. While some studies (Cornell + Numerator) indicate that GLP-1 users have significantly reduced grocery purchases by 5.5% (high-income families cutting 8.6%), with an 11% drop in snack sales in the six months after starting treatment.

And the data from this study actually reflects what General Mills has been experiencing in domestic sales.

As we can see in the table below, within its domestic market (which represents about 40% of the company's total revenue), between Fiscal 2025 (which ended in June of this year for General Mills) and Fiscal 2024, all segments from Meals & Baking, Morning Foods (cereal), and Snacks saw declines between 2% and 5%.

The situation is very dramatic for General Mills when compared to a year earlier, when domestic revenues came in at Fiscal 2023 ~$700 million higher and up 9% versus Fiscal 2022 (with snacks growing 13%).

Questionable Moves

Inevitably, companies that show a decline in revenue, mainly for structural reasons, as in the case of General Mills and other packaged food companies, are unlikely to see their stock price go anywhere other than south.

After all, we are talking about changes in eating habits. And changes for the sake of health, something that directly leads to an improvement in consumers' quality of life. It is logical, then, that General Mills' moat will disappear if it does not reinvent itself.

Assuming that structural headwinds are reversed with actionable measures, General Mills' management team has been betting on three fronts to overcome these headwinds: (1) diversification (pet food, organics, and premium snacks); (2) health-oriented reformulation and innovation; (3) international expansion.

The first guideline is aimed at expansion in pet food, a market not exposed to secular growth, which today represents 13% of the company's total revenues.

Next are the "better-for-you" segments, positioning themselves in clean-label snacks (Lärabar, EPIC, Food Should Taste Good) with attempts to rebrand classic products to "protein-boosted" versions or with reduced sugar/sodium.

Also, the focus on premium snacking, granola bars, and yogurt-based snacks, in addition to protein-rich yogurts (Yoplait, Go-Gurt) formats, attempts to ride the wave of healthy "on-the-go" foods.

The second also has to do with premiumization, with General Mills using repricing (inflation pass-through + premium formats) to sustain margins even with flat to negative volumes, seeking greater presence in club stores (Costco, Sam's, etc.), where private labels are a threat, but turnover is faster.

And finally, although the vast majority of revenues come from North America, there is an expansion of presence in Asia and LATAM, where snack/cereal penetration is still growing.

Honestly, I don't believe these initiatives will convince investors that the growth pace can return to pre-Fiscal 2025 levels. So much so that the consensus for top-line growth for Fiscal 2026 and beyond is poor, anticipating growth above 2.1% only in 2028.

Not All Bad: Economic Value Persists

While General Mills' performance and prospects appear to be underwhelming, there are still compelling elements for a defensive stock.

Even though the growth story seems not just stalled but actually declining, General Mills has historically been a strong generator of returns on invested capital (ROIC), a trend common in the processed food industry. The latest data shows an ROIC of 9.78% for General Mills, slightly below the company’s five-year historical average of 11%.

Using an average WACC of ~6%, the current ROIC still exceeds it by ~378 bps, which implies that General Mills is still creating economic value above its cost of capital. In other words, this shows that in a scenario of sluggish growth (eventually converging to stable), the business continues to generate returns above those required by shareholders and creditors, with the ROIC metric above WACC serving as a cushion against value destruction. So, yes, General Mills is still a defensive staple.

However, the spread here is not that high when compared to the spread between ROIC and WACC (approximated for each peer) of the main peers among packaged food staples. This implies that additional margin compression or structural volume loss could quickly lead to returns close to the cost of capital.

On Valuations

As it turns out, General Mills continues to create rather than destroy value for its shareholders, and the stock is also priced at attractive levels based on its operations.

A particularly useful, and often overlooked, metric for mature, capital-intensive companies such as General Mills is earnings yield, calculated from operating income (EBTI) divided by enterprise value.

Throughout fiscal 2025, General Mills generated $3.36 billion in operating income and currently trades at an enterprise value of $41.28 billion ($15.3 billion is debt), corresponding to an earnings yield of 8.1%, which is higher than the WACC of ~6%. Thus, the spread generated confirms that the company has created economic value for shareholders and has been at attractive valuation levels.

Again, looking at its peers, GIS is among the most attractively priced companies (based on its economic value created), although not as attractive as Kraft Heinz (KHC), Campbell's (CPB), and Conagra Brands (CAG).

Risks and Limits to Recovery

Even though operational metrics still confirm significant value creation for shareholders, the amount of value generated does not seem to offset the size of the headwinds facing General Mills, which virtually the entire packaged food staples industry shares.

In a way, General Mills checks many of the boxes for a value pick, as it manages to generate returns above its cost of capital and, at current depressed valuations, appears to be cheap. The company continues to pay regular dividends, and even without significant growth, over time, the market tends to recognize stable, dividend-paying companies (especially in the event of a reversal of the tech bull market), limiting GIS's decline. And I believe this is the main risk to the bearish thesis.

However, it seems unrealistic to take any position other than bearish on a company—or even an entire sector—that is facing mounting structural headwinds, including evolving consumer eating habits, the rise of GLP-1 drugs, and increased competition from private-label brands. In my view, as long as there are no clear signs that the company is adapting to these changes in consumer behavior, and as long as inflation and economic growth remain uncertain, I don’t see much chance of a meaningful turnaround. On the contrary, I expect these challenges to continue affecting performance at least through H2, and potentially into next year as well.

data.title: Fidelity Select Industrials Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

For the second quarter of 2025, the industrials sector advanced 13.51%, as measured by the MSCI U.S. IMI Industrials 25/50 Index, while the broad-based S&P 500® index had a gain of 10.94%.

Within the S&P 500, industrials ended with the third-best result for the quarter among the 11 market sectors. In a risk-on quarter from April 9 onward, the strongest stocks were concentrated in the information technology and communication services sectors. Nevertheless, a healthy pace of global spending on infrastructure and reshoring helped lift stocks in the industrials sector, as did an ongoing recovery in air traffic, which helped aerospace & defense shares. Relative to the broader S&P 500, the MSCI sector index performed about in line during April. May saw the MSCI index outperform, before giving back some of that excess performance in June. Like the S&P 500, the MSCI index ended June at an all-time high after erasing a brief but steep early-April decline that followed President Trump's announcement of a sweeping tariff plan. The historically fast rebound was set off by a 90-day pause on most planned tariffs, and sustained by robust corporate profits, a solid economy and inflation trending around the Federal Reserve's 2% target.

Looking at a few of the largest groups in the MSCI index, aerospace & defense (+21%) delivered a standout result. The industry has been recovering since the depths of the pandemic, as jet production attempts to catch up with order backlogs extending through the end of the decade. A key factor here was jet manufacturer Boeing (BA), which posted a 23% gain for the quarter, as well as securing a $20 billion contract for the F-47 from the U.S. Air Force's Next Generation Air Dominance program.

Elsewhere, industrial machinery & supplies & components recorded a more muted rise of about 5%, while building products was up 14%. The latter reflected a still-stalled U.S. housing market suffering from the twin headwinds of high home prices and elevated mortgage rates. Electrical components & equipment, another important group in the index, rose a strong 28%, while construction machinery & heavy transportation equipment was higher by 13%. Caterpillar (CAT) (+18%) was a standout in the latter group. ■

Performance Review

For the second quarter, the fund gained 20.05%, handily topping both the fund's sector benchmark, the MSCI U.S. IMI Industrials 25/50 Index, and the S&P 500®.

Versus the MSCI sector index, a large overweight in heavy electrical equipment due entirely to GE Vernova (GEV), one of the fund's largest holdings, meaningfully contributed to performance. Stock selection in aerospace & defense also helped.

Shares of GE Vernova gained roughly 74% the past three months, as the power-generation business that split from General Electric (GE) in April 2024 continued to fare well as a stand-alone entity. In April, the company reported quarterly financial results that topped analysts' expectations. Management held steady its financial forecast for Q2, noting the uncertain impact of tariffs and the company's efforts to mitigate it.

Overweighting Howmet Aerospace, another of the portfolio's top holdings, also was timely. The stock gained about 44% for the three months, as the maker of jet engine components has been riding a post-COVID recovery wave in air travel. After achieving record financial results in 2024, the company reported more good news for Q1 of 2025, with record revenue up 6%, an all-time high in profit and $125 million deployed for stock repurchases. Beyond aerospace, Howmet has made a meaningful push into industrial gas turbines, a market driven by surging global demand for electricity, especially from AI-powered data centers.

Detractors from performance were relatively muted this quarter, but investment choices in trading companies & distributors were one weak point. Picks in electrical components & equipment and construction materials also disappointed. In stock-specific terms, Ingersoll Rand (IR) was the portfolio's leading relative detractor, rising only 4% and failing to keep pace with the MSCI index. In May, the company reported first-quarter results that included missing estimates for both revenue and earnings. With that said, we added a bit to the position, as we continued to like the company's long-term prospects. ■

Outlook and Positioning

U.S. manufacturing surveys continued to be depressed during the quarter. For example, the ISM manufacturing index registered 49.0 for June. Since the fourth quarter of 2022, this index has mostly remained below 50, which is the dividing line between expanding and contracting manufacturing activity. January and February 2025 were exceptions that saw the index top 50 by a narrow margin, but it slipped back into contracting territory in March. This and similar surveys have been in depressed territory for quite a while, and yet the U.S. economy has not fallen into a recession. After such a protracted period of weak readings, we think it makes sense to look for areas of the industrials sector that could be poised for improvement.

The fund ended June with its largest overweight in aerospace & defense, as we continue to see strong prospects in that area. After considerable delays and problems with quality control, Boeing is having some success ramping up production, but backlogs will continue to be a fact of life, at least for the foreseeable future. This means that older planes will be kept in service longer, requiring more frequent and more expensive repairs and maintenance. Thus, two of the fund's largest overweights within this category at midyear were Howmet Aerospace, mentioned earlier, and GE Aerospace, which makes and repairs jet engines. We also carried an outsized position in TransDigm Group (TDG), a specialist in aftermarket parts, systems and subsystems for the aerospace market. Boeing itself remained an overweight as well.

Heavy electrical equipment also figured among the fund's largest industry overweights at quarter end. In this case, the portfolio's positioning was due to a sizable stake in one stock: GE Vernova, the fund's No. 1 overweight at the end of June. The company is a global leader in the electric power industry; its largest division makes gas turbines, which are used to generate electricity. Amid the broader global push toward electrification and decarbonization – including supplying the world's increased power needs due to the advent of generative artificial intelligence – Vernova is well positioned in our view.

Industrial machinery & supplies & components was also a significant overweight in the portfolio on June 30, given our expectations for improving demand there in a normalizing economy. Several noteworthy overweights in this group included Ingersoll Rand, ITT, Parker Hannifin (PH) and Dover.

Human resource & employment services ended the quarter as our top industry underweight. Individual stocks tied to this positioning included Automatic Data Processing (ADP) and Paychex (PAYX), two payroll processors in the MSCI index that the fund didn't own. Other underweighted segments included air freight & logistics, environmental & facilities services, and construction machinery & heavy transportation equipment. All were groups where we treaded lightly in favor of other groups in which we saw stronger growth potential or better valuations.

The portfolio's top-three individual overweights as of June 30 were GE Vernova, Howmet Aerospace and GE Aerospace. The three largest underweights at quarter-end were Honeywell (HON), RTX and Automatic Data Processing.

As always, we thank you for your confidence in Fidelity's investment-management capabilities. ■

Fidelity® Select Industrials Portfolio Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Select Industrials Portfolio is a sector-based equity-focused strategy that seeks to outperform its benchmark through active management.
  • We believe that uncertainty provides investment opportunity. Stock prices can become disengaged from a company's intrinsic (fair) value at cyclical extremes because of investor overreaction.
  • Cyclical stocks exhibit repeatable patterns. We believe this can present opportunities for a disciplined investor whose time horizon spans the next cycle.
  • We strive to capitalize on these opportunities through intensive, academic-style research on cyclical drivers and company-specific fundamentals, along with disciplined portfolio construction. The fund's success is predicated on finding the right mix of undervalued cyclicals and more-consistent earnings growers throughout the cycle.
  • Sector strategies could be used by investors as alternatives to individual stocks for either tactical- or strategic-allocation purposes.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater (leSs) than 1 is more (leSs) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return (fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index. R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance. It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns (the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month "risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk. The three month "risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

data.title: Q2 GDP Second Estimate: Real GDP At 3.3%, Higher Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The U.S. economy expanded in the second quarter, posting a strong rebound from earlier in the year. The BEA's second estimate of real gross domestic product showed economic growth increased at an annual rate of 3.3% in Q2 2025. The latest estimate was higher than the 3.0% forecast and marked a significant turn around from the Q1 final estimate of -0.5%.

Here is the opening text from the Bureau of Economic Analysis news release:

Real gross domestic product (GDP) increased at an annual rate of 3.3 percent in the second quarter of 2025 (April, May, and June), according to the second estimate released by the U.S. Bureau of Economic Analysis. In the first quarter, real GDP decreased 0.5 percent. The increase in real GDP in the second quarter primarily reflected a decrease in imports, which are a subtraction in the calculation of GDP, and an increase in consumer spending. These movements were partly offset by decreases in investment and exports.

Gross Domestic Product (GDP)

Real gross domestic product (GDP) measures how fast or slowly the economy is growing and measures the inflation-adjusted value of all goods and services produced by the economy. It is considered the broadest measure of economic activity and the primary indicator of an economy's health.

The Bureaus of Economic Analysis (BEA) releases real GDP data on a monthly basis. There are three versions released a month apart: advance, second, and final, each incorporating data that was previously unavailable. Economists can use GDP to determine whether an economy is growing or experiencing a recession.

This chart illustrates the annualized, inflation-adjusted change in U.S. gross domestic product (GDP) from the preceding quarter, with data beginning in Q2 1947. (Prior to Q2 1947, GDP was calculated annually.) For context, the chart also highlights recessions (as determined by the National Bureau of Economic Research, NBER), the historical GDP growth average of 3.20%, and the current 10-year moving average of 2.63%.

A key trend is evident: The 10-year moving average of GDP growth fell below the historical average in 2007 and has remained there, signaling a significant deceleration in U.S. economic expansion since the Great Recession. Current growth exceeds both the long-term series average and the 10-year moving average, indicating a faster pace of growth than experienced historically.

The chart above has many interesting data points but can be quite overwhelming to look at. This next chart provides a simplified look over the past few years.

Here is a log-scale chart of real GDP with an exponential regression, which helps us understand growth cycles since the 1947 inception of quarterly GDP. The regression illustrates the fact that the current trend, since the Great Recession, has a visibly lower slope than the long-term trend. In fact, the current GDP is 12.9% below the pre-recession trend (2008).

A particularly telling representation of slowing growth in the US economy is the year-over-year rate of change. Beginning in 1947, the average year-over-year rate at the start of recessions is 3.2%, with a range of 1.28% to 6.78%. The current year-over-year rate for real GDP is at 2.07%. The current level is at or below 9 of the 12 recession starts during this time frame.

GDP: A Look Ahead

In the chart below, we use the Conference Board's Economic Forecast for the U.S. Economy to visualize the GDP forecast over the next few years.

August 08, 2025Tariff Reality to Weigh on Growth; Lower Uncertainty to Provide Offset

Higher tariffs are set to weigh on real GDP growth in H2 and into 2026, as consumers bear the brunt of higher prices. Businesses will navigate the tariff landscape with more clarity than was the case earlier this year. Even though tariffs could eat into their profit margins, accelerated depreciation, one of the key provisions in the latest fiscal legislation, could incentivize business investment in the coming months. TCB estimates the bulk of tariff impacts to affect Q4 and early 2026. Given this outlook, we anticipate the FOMC will likely resume cutting interest rates this December. A sharp worsening in labor market conditions is a downside risk for an earlier cut in either September or October. As the Fed continues to cut policy rates next year, growth is projected to rebound, but the extent of the recovery will likely be capped by social benefit cuts towards the end of 2026.

data.title: Altria: We Are Buyers Of The Stock For The Dividends
data.description:
Altria (NYSE:MO) manufactures and sells smokeable and oral tobacco products in the United States. The firm has a wide product portfolio, including iconic cigarette brands like Marlboro.

We started covering Altria back in August 2024, with a buy rating. We were bullish on Altria because of its attractive valuation based on dividend discount models as well as the growth potential offered by its NJOY product category.

Since our last writing, Altria outperformed the broader market significantly, taking the dividends into account.

We believe that Altria is likely to keep outperforming the broader market due to their appealing valuation at the current price point.

The aim of our article today is to give an updated view on why we believe the firm will keep outperforming despite the recent strong stock performance. To assess the valuation of MO, once gain using dividend discount models. We are also going to take a look at the latest developments around the firm, including news and key takeaways from the most recent earnings report, published at the end of July.

Valuation

In our previous writing, we discussed that MO is a reliable dividend payer, paying sustainable quarterly dividends for decades. Our view on MO as a dividend payer did not change since then. The firm kept paying quarterly dividends and also managed to increase them by 3.9%. For these reasons, we believe that the previously applied dividend discount model is still a valid approach to valuing MO's stock.

Earlier, we used a required rate of return of 7.1%, based on the company's weighted average cost of capital (WACC). According to the latest estimates, the WACC remained 7.1%, and for this reason, once again we are going to be using this value as our required rate of return for the calculation.

Last year, we used two dividend growth rate assumptions to come up with a range of fair values, one assumed a 0% growth rate in perpetuity, resulting in a fair value of $59 per share, while the other assumed a growth rate of 4.1% until 2030 and 2.5% beyond, yielding a fair value of $99 per share.

Let us revisit these assumptions now. The following table summarizes MO's dividend growth rates for the past years. We will be using this table to create three scenarios - low case, mid case and high case.

Low case

Just like before, for the low case we are going to use no growth - 0% in perpetuity. These assumptions yield us a fair value of $64 per share, which is roughly 4% below the current share price. While the tobacco industry is not a fast-growing industry, we believe that assuming 0% growth is too conservative.

Mid case

If we get just a bit more optimistic and assume no growth for the next 7 years, but assume a growth rate of 2% beyond, which is roughly equivalent to the overall growth of the US economy, we get a fair value of $81 per share, representing an upside of 22%. In our view, these assumptions are still too conservative. We see that there are certain segments in the tobacco industry that are growing, plus there is also some positive enforcement action against illicit e-vapor, which could lead to higher sales for MO.

High case

And last, our high case, results in a fair value of $102 per share, assuming a 2% growth in perpetuity. In our view, a 2% growth is by no means too optimistic. We believe this is an entirely realistic scenario as the firm in the last 5 to 10 years increased its dividends in a much higher pace.

For these reason, we believe that MO's stock at the current price level could be a very attractive buying opportunity for dividend- and dividend growth investors.

But before we put out our final verdict we have to look at some of the latest developments around to firm and the industry to understand whether the outperformance is likely to last or not.

Macro- and microeconomic considerations

Oral tobacco

First of all, let us look at the growth segment of MO, oral tobacco. We can see that the U.S. oral tobacco industry volume is growing at an increasing pace. This is also reflected in the on! shipment volume, which is one of MO's flagship products. We believe it is a good sign that MO is able to capitalize and effectively take part in this industry growth.

JUUL patent

In our previous article, we mentioned that NJOY might be fuelling MO's growth in the future. Unfortunately, there were problems JUUL's patents, but MO managed to react quickly and modified their product. NJOY ACE is now thought be addressing all disputed patents - namely 4. Looking forward, we believe this product category is still likely to fuel MO's growth in the long term.

Illicit e-vapor

Illicit e-vapor was and is a problem for the entire industry. Now, however, we are starting to see positive enforcement action from the FDA. This enforcement focuses primarily on importing vapor products.

Capital allocation

While we already talked extensively about dividends in our valuation section, we also have to highlight that MO is also allocating significant amount of capital for share repurchases. Altria repurchased 10.4 million shares in the previous quarter for $600 million and they are planning to spend another $400 million in this year for repurchases, further generating value for the shareholders. Please note that this upside is not included in our valuation model.

Outlook

MO recently adjusted the lower end of its 2025 financial outlook higher. With this adjustment an EPS growth rate of 3% to 5% is expected compared to the prior year.

We highlighted in our previous article MO's relative independence from the consumer sentiment, but we would like to emphasise it again here. While many firms are struggling with weak demand for their products, MO is well-positioned to withstand the negative impacts of low consumer confidence as the demand for their products is relatively inelastic. For this reason, we also believe that MO could be an attractive stock to own now.

Debt

Before we conclude our writing, there is one significant risk that we need to point out: debt. MO has significant amount of debt in its capital structure:

If the interest rates are to remain higher for an extended period, it could keep MO's interest expenses also high, potentially leading to a poorer financial performance.

Conclusion

After taking a look at MO's valuation, using updated input parameters for our dividend discount model, we can conclude that MO's stock appears to be undervalued. For this reason, we believe the stock could be an attractive buy for value-, dividend- and dividend growth investors at the current levels. This buy rating is in line with our previously assigned buy rating.

On top of dividends, MO also repurchased more than 10 million shares for $600 million in the previous quarter and they are planning to spend another $400 million till the end of the year.

MO is also effectively participating in the growth of the oral tobacco industry in the U.S. with its on! products. Also due to the nature of their product portfolio and the inelasticity of demand for these products, MO's financial performance is relatively independent from the consumer sentiment.

Positive enforcement action against illicit e-vapor, as well as an improved full-year outlook further support our bullish thesis.

data.title: Fidelity Select Health Care Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Health care stocks returned -5.80% in the second quarter, according to the MSCI U.S. IMI Health Care 25/50 Index. The sector significantly underperformed the 10.94% advance of the S&P 500® index as health care shares faced policy headwinds, rising costs and poor performance among some industry stalwarts. Meanwhile, the broader U.S. stock market gained strongly this quarter, ending June at an all-time high after erasing a brief but steep early-April decline that followed President Trump's announcement of a new tariff plan.

Still, performance was varied among the industries within the MSCI health care index. The managed care segment declined by far the most this quarter, returning -31%. Higher costs associated with Medicare Advantage as well as potential cuts to Medicaid funding weighed heavily on the industry. In particular, UnitedHealth Group (UNH) – a large index component at about 6% of assets – returned -40% the past three months. The stock fell on higher-than-expected medical costs stemming from patients' increased use of physicians and hospital outpatient services.

Life science tools & services (-8%) and pharmaceuticals (-7%) stocks also declined this quarter, as trade uncertainty loomed large on these industries. Here, diagnostics firms such as Thermo Fisher Scientific (TMO) (-18%) and pharma giant Bristol-Myers Squibb (BMY) (-23%) underperformed. The life science tools & services group was also hurt by decreased research funding at the National Institutes of Health, although these cuts were paused at midyear. Pharma shares were further crimped by the threat of lower drug costs.

Conversely, the strongest industry performer within the index was health care technology, which was propelled by investors' excitement over all things tech related. Shares of Veeva Systems (+24%), a provider of cloud-based services to the life sciences industry, were driven by strong quarterly financial results and the launch and development of its AI-powered platform. Health care facilities (+12%) and health care distributors (+11%) also gained. ■

Performance Review

For the second quarter of 2025, the fund returned -2.55%, outperforming the MSCI health care index.

Stock selection notably contributed to the fund's performance versus the MSCI health care index in Q2, especially in biotechnology and pharmaceuticals. Among individual stocks, the fund's top two relative contributors were long-held health care equipment names: Insulet (+19%) and Boston Scientific (BSX) (+7%). Shares of Insulet, a maker of insulin management devices for diabetics, rose sharply in May after the firm reported strong Q1 financial results on significant revenue growth for its flagship Omnipod device, a successful global rollout of its latest Omnipod 5 and international expansion. Meanwhile, Boston Scientific achieved strong growth this period, with particularly robust demand for its heart devices, including pacemakers and stents, which represent most of Boston Scientific's revenue. In April, management reported better-than-expected Q1 profit and sales, boosted by the cardiovascular and electrophysiology segments. We pared the positions in Boston Scientific and Insulet this quarter, yet they were our largest and ninth-largest positions, respectively, on June 30.

On the negative side, an overweight and stock picking in managed care weighed the most on relative performance, especially our sizable overweight in UnitedHealth Group (-40%), by far the fund's biggest relative detractor in Q2. The stock fell on April 17 after the managed health care and insurance giant announced disappointing quarterly financial results and slashed its guidance for 2025. We notably reduced the position this quarter. Still, UNH was the fund's No. 3 holding at quarter end. It also hurt to avoid index component and device maker Abbott Laboratories (ABT) (+3%). ■

Outlook and Positioning

We expect recent market volatility to persist until we have greater certainty in this Presidential administration's health care policies. This period many health care stocks were negatively impacted by concerns about possible cuts to Medicaid. Disenrollment in Medicaid would result in an increase in uninsured people, which would particularly weigh on health care facilities. In addition, a weaker U.S. dollar threatens the future earnings for companies with currency exposure, such as device makers Boston Scientific and Masimo (MASI). We remain long growth stocks, and this period has reinforced our conviction in the investment process and our portfolio positioning. While it's no surprise to the team, the importance of innovation continues to be reinforced by the market. Despite the macro uncertainty, large companies continue to deploy capital into acquisitions, and breakthrough clinical data is being rewarded by the market.

We are also in the early innings of structural transformation in the largest health care market in the world – the United States' $4 trillion health care economy. Winners and losers will emerge, and the pandemic has accelerated the pace of change. In the medical equipment segment, volumes are strong, new product launches are exciting, and the industry is insulated from much of the macro uncertainty in the sector. We expect earnings growth to recover in the managed health care and health care services spaces following this challenging period, and once we gain clarity on the impending budget cuts at the National Institutes of Health – an important driver of the industries' performance. We materially decreased our stake in managed care this quarter, largely due to our substantial paring of UnitedHealth Group in the fund based macro uncertainty.

We also reduced our position in health care equipment in Q2, given its relatively strong performance, and invested more in biotechnology, where cyclical assets were beaten down due to tariff concerns. In biotech, companies have delivered positive data on breakthrough drugs. The outlook for the pharma industry is murky given the impact tariffs will have on the space. The next milestone, in our view, will be the trade deal with the European Union due to the massive amount of pharma manufacturing that runs through Ireland. In late June, the U.S. and China came to a trade agreement, which was a positive for life science & tools stocks with exposure to China. We expect a stronger environment for this industry in 2026, when we think earnings guidance will improve. There are four major risks to our outlook:(1) the U.S. fiscal situation beyond the debt limit antics is untenable. Structural change is required for entitlement programs;(2) enactment or perceived risks of legislation that either impairs the industry's pricing power or movement toward a single-payer system would be a material negative and have many long-term consequences;(3) continuation of the defensive rotation in the market, given the uncertain economic outlook and the direction of interest rates and inflation;(4) emerging-markets growth is becoming an increasingly important exposure. To the extent we see protectionism and/or foreign-exchange volatility, this can create unintended consequences in the short-to-intermediate term.

Going forward, we plan to invest in the most innovative areas of the health care sector, where the team's insights can deliver material long-term value. In particular, we are excited about areas like value-based care, "consumer-health tech," cell therapy and medical equipment manufacturers that are beginning to hit an inflection point of profitability. ■

Fidelity® Select Health Care Portfolio Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Select Health Care Portfolio is a sector-based, equity-focused strategy that seeks to outperform its benchmark through active management.
  • Stock picking is the core of our investment process and relies on fundamental, bottom-up research. We look to leverage Fidelity's deep and experienced global health care team in building a broad portfolio of health care companies, ranging from high-quality, stable earnings growers to opportunistic names producing innovative products that disrupt the marketplace.
  • Our fundamental analysis focuses on free cash flow and capital allocation, which we believe are the biggest drivers of long-term shareholder value, and also examines market opportunity, sales growth and margin outlook.
  • We seek to concentrate the portfolio in our best ideas, but carefully manage risk through position sizing.
  • Sector strategies could be used by investors as alternatives to individual stocks for either tactical- or strategic-allocation purposes.

data.title: Fidelity Select Financials Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The past three months, the financials sector, as measured by the MSCI U.S. IMI Financials 5% Capped Linked Index, gained 6.91%, while the S&P 500® index advanced 10.94%. Stocks entered Q2 in a tailspin, as post-election optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new U.S. presidential administration, disruption to government programs and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities.

However, after a brief but steep early-April decline that followed President Trump's announcement of a sweeping tariff plan, the S&P 500 embarked on a rapid recovery, ending June at an all-time high. The historically fast rebound was set off by a 90-day pause on most planned tariffs, and sustained by robust corporate profits, a solid economy and inflation trending around the Federal Reserve's 2% target. These tailwinds overshadowed rising tension in the Middle East and lingering uncertainty about trade ahead of the July 8 expiration of the tariff pause.

Against this backdrop, financials posted the fifth-best result of the 11 sectors in the S&P 500®. The top-performing industry group in the sector, by far, was investment banking & brokerage (+25%). A surging stock market, including avid participation by retail investors, along with expectations of a smoother path for corporate mergers & acquisitions, were factors supporting this group. Consumer finance stocks (+19%) also outperformed amid resilient consumer spending.

Diversified banks, the largest industry group in the MSCI index, gained about 14% in the quarter, while regional banks, which tend to be smaller institutions, were up 6%. Residual uneasiness about the financial stability of regional banks – likely a holdover from the failure of several lenders within that industry in 2023 – led investors to favor their larger, more-diversified peers. We'll also highlight asset management & custody banks (+12%), which did well in an environment of increasing stock prices, a positive backdrop for growth in fees based on assets under management.

On the other hand, several insurance groups lagged the MSCI sector index, as they are considered defensive holdings, which were out of favor for most of this quarter. Groups affected by this phenomenon included property & casualty insurance (-2%), life & health insurance (-3%) and insurance brokers (-9%). ■

Performance Review

For the three months ending June 30, 2025, the fund gained 8.15%, outpacing the MSCI U.S. IMI Financials 5% Capped Linked Index, but trailing the broadly based S&P 500® index.

Versus the MSCI sector index, avoiding the weak-performing multi-sector holdings category made a meaningful contribution this quarter. That was primarily due to lack of exposure to Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.B), which returned roughly -9% for the three months, declining sharply in early April on tariff-related uncertainty and the potential impact on the insurance-focused conglomerate's business and vast investment portfolio. The stock recovered until May 3, when Warren Buffett announced his plan to step down as CEO at year-end, setting off a decline for the stock that lasted through midyear. He will remain chairman of the company, which posted quarterly financial results that fell short of estimates, including a 14% decrease in operating earnings. Profit from insurance underwriting was a particularly weak spot. In the asset management & custody industry, overweights in Northern Trust (NTRS) (+29%) and State Street (+20%) both contributed, aided by the prospects for rising fees.

On the negative side, stock selection in the investment banking & brokerage category notably detracted in Q2, as did picks in transaction & payment processing services. Within the first group, avoiding Goldman Sachs (GS) Group (+30%) hurt most. In April, the bank reported that Q1 earnings increased 15% and revenue rose 6% from the same quarter a year earlier. Growth was driven by higher net revenues in global banking and markets. We like the company but thought there were better opportunities elsewhere.

Not having a position in Robinhood Markets (HOOD) also hurt. The stock of the financial services firm providing a commission-free, electronic trading platform to investors advanced 125% the past three months. On April 30, the company announced Q1 results that included a 50% increase in revenue and a 114% rise in net income. Then, late in the quarter, Robinhood launched an extensive range of new cryptocurrency products that marked a significant push into the crypto space and further boosted the stock. With that said, the stock seemed overvalued to us. ■

Outlook and Positioning

After another quarter where the Fed stood pat on interest rates, we think the chances are good that we will see one or two rate cuts in the second half of the year. With that said, we still don't know what the tariff landscape will look like when all the deals with U.S. trading partners are done. The threat of a tariff-induced worsening of inflation could easily delay further rate-cutting by the central bank, which is a concern.

As of quarter end, the fund's largest industry overweight was regional banks. We continue to find good value in this group, based on our view of a gradually normalizing economy and investor failure to clearly distinguish between the stronger companies and the weaker ones, following the 2023 regional bank crisis. This is especially pertinent, in our view, now that valuations in so many parts of the stock market have rebounded so quickly off the April low. Good value is increasingly harder to find.

Popular (BPOP) and Associated Banc-Corp are two overweight fund holdings in the regional banking group. We view both lenders as lower on the risk scale, compared with others that were hit hardest during the regional banking crisis, and they therefore have seen their stocks lag somewhat during the recent recovery phase. Both have solid deposit bases and room for adjusting their balance sheets over time, which should enable them to reap the benefits of higher interest rates. Popular is the leading depository bank in Puerto Rico, a market with only a handful of significant lenders.

Another meaningful industry overweight as of quarter end was diversified financial services. There was no real unifying theme here, other than that we saw favorable opportunities in all three of the fund's holdings in the group: Apollo Global Management (APO), Corebridge Financial (CRBG) and Voya Financial (VOYA).

Elsewhere, the fund maintained a significant overweight in reinsurance – specifically, Reinsurance Group of America, the fund's second-biggest overweight at the end of June. As a reinsurer, RGA shares risk on individual and group life and health insurance policies in exchange for premiums. By assuming risk, RGA empowers its insurer clients to limit potential losses and to deliver on their promises to pay policyholders' claims. The reinsurer had a difficult time during the pandemic, but its business continues to normalize, and we like this stock longer term.

Overall, the largest individual overweight in the portfolio during the quarter was payment processor Mastercard, a favorite of ours for its solid brand recognition and greater exposure to lucrative cross-border transactions.

On the other hand, one of the fund's biggest industry underweights at quarter end was financial exchanges & data. Individual underweights of note in this group were S&P Global (SPGI), Intercontinental Exchange (ICE) and CME Group (CME), none of which were in the portfolio during the quarter. Companies in this category tend to be somewhat defensive, and we thought there were better opportunities in other groups.

As always, we thank you for your confidence in our stewardship of the fund, and in Fidelity's investment-management capabilities. ■

Fidelity® Select Financials Portfolio Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Select Financials Portfolio is a sector-based, equity-focused strategy that seeks to outperform its benchmark through active management.
  • The fund focuses on higher-quality companies, as measured by return on equity, which we believe can drive robust growth and create more-attractive risk-adjusted returns. Additionally, the fund targets improving businesses where such improvement is underappreciated by the market and allows the company to transition to a more profitable franchise in the future.
  • We look for firms with an attractive valuation relative to the sector index, and believe combining high quality and low valuation should compound returns over time with lower risk of multiple compression, as well as less downside capture.
  • Stock selection and idea generation come from fundamental, bottom-up research that leverages the deep and experienced global financials team at Fidelity. We consider attractive financial stocks outside of the benchmark that offer the potential for favorable risk-adjusted returns.
  • Sector strategies could be used by investors as alternatives to individual stocks for either tactical- or strategic-allocation purposes.

data.title: Fidelity Select Energy Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The energy sector returned -7.34% the past three months, as measured by the MSCI U.S. IMI Energy 25/50 Index. Broadly speaking, stocks entered Q2 in a tailspin, as post-election optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new U.S. presidential administration, disruption to government programs and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities.

However, after a brief but steep early-April decline that followed President Trump's announcement of a sweeping tariff plan, the S&P 500 embarked on a rapid recovery, ending June at an all-time high. The historically fast rebound was set off by a 90-day pause on most planned tariffs, and sustained by robust corporate profits, a solid economy and inflation trending around the Federal Reserve's 2% target. These tailwinds overshadowed rising tension in the Middle East and lingering uncertainty about trade ahead of the July 8 expiration of the tariff pause.

Amid this macroeconomic backdrop, crude-oil prices trended downward after hitting a quarterly high of $75 per barrel in mid-June. From start to finish, crude oil declined from about 9%, from $71.47 per barrel to $64.79 bbl this quarter, according to the West Texas Intermediate spot price, a benchmark of U.S. oil prices. Still, it remained at a level that continued to support profitability for many companies in the sector. Meanwhile, Henry Hub natural gas prices stayed in a range of about $3 per Metric Million British Thermal Unit during the quarter. The price of this commodity remained historically low, however, which continued to be somewhat of a headwind to profit growth for natural gas-related companies.

Among the larger industries within the MSCI sector index, oil & gas equipment & services firms, which represented about 9% of the sector index, fared the worst, returning -13% this quarter. In this group, Halliburton (HAL) (-19%) and Schlumberger (SLB) (-18%) were notable detractors. Elsewhere, integrated oil & gas companies the largest group at about 39% of the index, returned about -11%. Here, Occidental Petroleum (OXY) (-14%) and Chevron (-13%) underperformed. In contrast, oil & gas refining & marketing (+7%) was the only industry with a gain. ■

Performance Review

For the second quarter of 2025, the fund returned -4.01%, outpacing the MSCI U.S. IMI Energy 25/50 Index but lagging the broad-based S&P 500 index.

Turning to the fund, out-of-index exposure to the independent power producers & energy traders category notably contributed to outperformance of the sector index in Q2. In this group, owning a sizable stake in power producer Vistra (VST), which advanced about 65%, added value. In early May, the company reported better-than-expected adjusted earnings for the first quarter, partly due to higher electricity sales. Also, management affirmed its financial guidance for the remainder of 2025 and repurchased $337 million in outstanding shares of Vistra stock. Vistra was among the fund's top holdings at quarter end.

The fund also benefited from its underweight in Chevron (-13%), a weak-performing and sizable index component. In May, Chevron reported slightly better-than-consensus first-quarter earnings, though the company reported rising debt and that the rate of share repurchases was below the market's expectations.

Conversely, underweighting the oil & gas storage & transportation (-2%) segment detracted from the fund's relative result this quarter, as did not having exposure to the small coal & consumable fuels (+30%) category. Among individual stocks, an underweight in Williams Companies (WMB), an index component from the oil & gas storage & transportation group that gained 6%, was the fund's biggest relative detractor. In early May, the natural gas producer and distributor reported better-than-expected Q1 earnings, driven in part by the rising price of gas in Q1 and solid corporate execution.

Not owning Kinder Morgan (KMI) (+4%) from the same category also meaningfully hurt. ■

Outlook and Positioning

As of midyear, we expect oil prices to remain conducive to energy companies' corporate profitability – likely in the $65 to $90 per barrel range − barring any major changes in the environment, such as a global recession influenced by new tariff policies. Looking ahead, the key drivers of oil prices include rising global demand, slowing U.S. supply growth as shale drilling matures, production restraint by the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting Countries as key member Saudi Arabia seeks to maintain high oil prices, and elevated geopolitical risk due to ongoing conflict in the Middle East and Ukraine.

Meanwhile, U.S. natural gas prices are likely to remain under pressure due to healthy domestic production, further delays in the development of U.S. LNG export terminals and modest growth in demand in this country. Generally speaking, industry refining margins are a bit of a wildcard due to the uncertain timing of new capacity additions, offset by refinery retirements in the U.S. and Europe. However, we believe U.S. refining margins should remain structurally higher than they historically have due to low U.S. natural gas prices and crude-oil costs relative to non-U.S. refiners.

The risk of increasing geopolitical tension in the Middle East could lead to higher oil and gas prices, especially if production from key players, including Iran, Saudi Arabia, UAE, Qatar or Iraq, is disrupted by regional security issues.

Weakening global economic growth, particularly in either the U.S. and/or China, could lead to slowing demand for oil and natural gas. Longer term, however, we'll note that increasing investments will be needed to meet what we believe will be rising demand for these energy sources in the coming years and decades. Elsewhere, the increasing need for electricity stemming from reshoring, electrification trends and the build-out of new data centers should be an important trend in the power industry.

As of June 30, the fund's most prominent industry overweight is among oil & gas equipment & services stocks because we believe the outlook for capital spending in this segment is poised to improve. Additionally, this is an industry that we believe should benefit from multiple years of growing investments needed to support rising demand for these crucial commodities. We also believe many investors are underestimating the need to meet the world's growing demand for oil and gas, as well as the upside of the upcoming business cycle for oilfield services firms. Moreover, there have been several years of low investment in global markets, which recently has reversed course. The fund's biggest individual overweight positions in this group as of year-end are TechnipFMC (FTI), National Energy Services Reunited and SLB. The fund also had a moderate overweight in oil and gas refining & marketing stocks. Here, the fund's largest overweights at midyear were Marathon ■ Petroleum and Valero Energy (VLO), both among the top 10 holdings.

Fidelity® Select Energy Portfolio Investment Approach

  • Fidelity® Select Energy Portfolio is a sector-based, equity-focused strategy that seeks to outperform its benchmark through active management.
  • We believe stocks can become mispriced relative to intrinsic value for a variety of reasons, including cyclically depressed earnings or overly positive or negative sentiment.
  • Supported by in-depth fundamental research, we seek to uncover investment opportunities by analyzing the drivers of supply and demand for energy commodities, in combination with valuations and cash-generation potential for energy stocks.
  • Our process is grounded in the belief that the ability to generate free cash flow over time is the best barometer of value, and that companies with quality businesses trading at attractive levels compared with future free-cash-flow generation tend to outperform.
  • Sector strategies could be used by investors as alternatives to individual stocks for either tactical- or strategic-allocation purposes.

data.title: Fidelity Select Consumer Staples Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

The consumer staples sector gained about 1% in the second quarter, according to the MSCI U.S. IMI Consumer Staples 25/50 Index. This result notably underperformed the 10.94% return of the broad-based S&P 500® index.

Stocks entered Q2 in a tailspin as post-election optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new U.S. presidential administration, disruption to government programs and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities. However, after a brief but steep early-April decline that followed President Trump's announcement of a sweeping tariff plan, the S&P 500 embarked on a rapid recovery, ending June at an all-time high. The historically fast rebound was set off by a 90-day pause on most planned tariffs, and sustained by robust corporate profits, a solid economy and inflation trending around the Federal Reserve's 2% target. These tailwinds overshadowed rising tension in the Middle East and lingering uncertainty about trade ahead of the July 8 expiration of the tariff pause.

Amid this backdrop, the MSCI sector index held steady most of the quarter, gaining 0.94% in April – despite the sharp market decline early that month – and 1.66% in May. However, the sector lost steam in June, returning -1.88%. As the broader market rebounded over the course of the three months, staples stocks moved from outperforming (in April) to underperforming (in May and June) as investors shifted away from defensive sectors and toward more growth-oriented sectors and stocks.

Among the biggest segments in the sector index, household products, making up 17% of the benchmark, returned -7%, hindered by large index components Kimberly Clark (-9%) and Church & Dwight (CHD) (-12%). Packaged food & meats (-7%) and soft drinks & non-alcoholic beverages (-3%), together making up about 30% of the index, also dragged on the sector's result. Underperformers in packaged foods included General Mills (GIS) (-12%), Tyson Foods (TSN) (-12%) and The Campbell's Company (CPB) (-22%), while soft drinks was hindered by PepsiCo (PEP) (-11%). Conversely, consumer staples merchandise retail – which accounted for 29% of the index in Q2 – gained 8%, helped by Walmart (WMT) (+12%) and discount retailers Dollar General (DG) (+31%) and Dollar Tree (DLTR) (+32%). ■

Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares returned -3.28%, lagging the 0.69% advance of the MSCI U.S. IMI Consumer Staples 25/50 Index.

Security selection and industry positioning both detracted from the fund's performance versus the sector index for the quarter, especially picks in the household products and packaged foods & meats segments, as well as a large underweight in consumer staples merchandise retail.

From the household products group, an outsized position in Energizer (-32%) was the biggest individual relative detractor. Energizer's shares fell this period, despite solid quarterly results, after the battery maker cut its full-year adjusted earnings per share forecast, citing a more cautious consumer. We view Energizer as a value play for the fund, as the stock is currently quite cheap despite steady financial performance. We see turnaround potential here and added to the holding.

From the tobacco group, a large underweight in Philip Morris International (PM), best known for its Marlboro cigarettes, was the second-biggest individual relative detractor. The stock gained 16% this quarter, driven by sales strength for the company's smokeless, oral-pouch product Zyn. We meaningfully reduced exposure to the stock, which was the No. 2 underweight as of June 30, as we see increasing competition amid oral nicotine pouch products.

Conversely, picks in personal care products helped most. From this segment, an overweight stake in beauty-product manufacturer Estee Lauder (EL) (+23%) was the top contributor to relative performance. We had been negative on Estee Lauder in the past, predicting a business downturn due to sluggish growth in China's luxury market. However, after the stock declined significantly this year, we began to buy in, anticipating a cyclical recovery. That recovery has started to take shape and the fund benefited this quarter. We reduced the holding by quarter end to take some profits. ■

Outlook and Positioning

As Q3 begins, we believe business dynamics within the consumer staples sector have mostly returned to normal following post-pandemic doldrums, though we are keeping a close eye on how tariff policies may shift the landscape. Aside from that, we see the sector remaining a defensive anchor amid the current uncertainty. Trends in the sector are not particularly strong, but they are not disastrous. The recent significant headwinds related to the high cost of raw materials and commodities are largely in the past, even with ongoing uncertainty related to tariffs. There are exceptions, however, including commodities such as cocoa and coffee, which are expected to see continued rising prices if tariffs are implemented as expected. With that said, we are excited about the potential to drive the fund's relative result with stock picking, given the sector's rich landscape, including wide valuation spreads, which may present opportunities to capitalize on turnaround stories or undervalued stocks.

We believe this environment may allow consumer staples stocks to perform favorably versus the broader market through the second half of the year, especially if the market landscape becomes more pessimistic. In managing the fund, we plan to continue to focus on core fundamentals and traditional industry drivers, with a preference for companies operating in a favorable market structure and maintaining a strong underlying growth profile. For example, we believe Coca-Cola and Procter & Gamble could benefit from consistent, if unexciting, financial delivery against lower expectations. The two are the fund's top holdings as of June 30. Since taking office on January 20, President Trump has rolled out an aggressive, unpredictable and ever-changing tariff plan. Its effect on the consumer staples sector has been varied through the end of June, and it's too early to assess the longer-term impact.

Tariffs on Chinese imports, however, have already started to bite. Although incremental, these tariffs have driven up consumer electronics prices, with retailers such as Walmart and Target in line to feel the brunt of the impact. Tariffs on aluminum also have led to price increases in canned goods and aluminum foil. Looking further down the line at the potential impact on the consumer staples sector, we believe Trump's tariff policies could increase input costs and consumer prices.

While this scenario is negative for consumer staples in absolute terms, it likely would position the sector as a relative safe haven compared with growth sectors in the event of an economic downturn. We saw this demonstrated during the early-April market decline, when staples stocks outperformed the broader market. The key will be how companies navigate these hurdles by managing costs and pricing strategies in response to the evolving tariff landscape. This is something we are monitoring and discussing with the business leaders we visit.

Regardless of the market backdrop, the fund's positioning and our core investment strategy are largely unchanged. We continue to focus on choosing relative "winners" and "losers" within each industry. This stock selection process is based on building robust financial and operating models that we believe give us an edge in forecasting earnings revisions for companies. We supplement this fundamental work with industry-level analysis to get ahead of broader trends that could influence the sector.

The past three months, we maintained the fund's large overweight positions in soft drinks & non-alcoholic beverages and distillers & vintners. We believe the beverages segment, particularly non-alcoholic beverages, is the best market structure in consumer staples, based on its high barriers to entry, relatively tepid competitive pressure and fragmented customer base. ■

Fidelity® Select Consumer Staples Portfolio

Investment Approach

• Fidelity® Select Consumer Staples Portfolio is a sector-based, equity-focused strategy that seeks to outperform its benchmark through active management.

• Our core philosophy is that stock prices follow earnings and returns over the long term. We look for durable franchises capable of compounding value and delivering above-average earnings growth over time.

• Investment opportunities arise when there is a differentiated view with regard to the long-term earnings power of a company. We focus on stocks where the market underestimates the magnitude or duration of growth and the valuation is attractive.

• Fundamental research is key to our approach, as we build the portfolio stock by stock and take a multiyear investment perspective to get a differentiated view on long-term performance drivers. We also look to capitalize on short-term market opportunities, with the goal of optimizing risk-adjusted returns for shareholders.

• Sector strategies could be used by investors as alternatives to individual stocks for either tactical- or strategic-allocation purposes.

data.title: Nasdaq Leads U.S. Indices In Indecisive Moves Post Nvidia Earnings: Dow Jones, S&P 500, Nasdaq Overview (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Elior Manier

US indices are rising but in an unassertive fashion, and the same has happened throughout the whole week.

The dovish interpretation from Powell's speech last Friday definitely helped to sustain bullish momentum, with the S&P 500 timidly breaking new highs in yesterday's session and the Dow Jones breaking through its previous record (current all-time highs at 45,757).

However, it appears that markets are awaiting the most influential piece of data, releasing on the 5th of September - the infamous Non-Farm Payrolls report.

This week's indecisive trading is typical of the last week of August due to many participants being off their screens and not much key data to keep the key players from doing so.

Yesterday's post-close Nvidia (NVDA) release sent mixed signs, but it appears that technicals still corroborate a potential upside. NVDA is still down around 0.90% on the session, with what seems to be profit-taking flows. Some dip-buying is currently ongoing.

Some key technical patterns are still coming into play, which should influence trading ahead of tomorrow's Core PCE release.

A broad look at US Equities

US Indices technical analysis: Dow Jones, S&P 500 and Nasdaq 4H charts

Dow Jones: Double top coming into play?

Our previous analysis of US indices had mentioned a break retest technical pattern that tends to bring continued upside and more sustainability to trends. However, the landmark Dow is down about 0.20% in today's session after marking an intermediate double top.

Inflows are going towards other sectors, but the picture is very mixed, leaving the most logical reason behind the selling being normal profit-taking. The price action is still holding an upward channel, with the previous selling happening at its upper bound.

Indeed, a lack of activity may prevent pushing for further highs, particularly ahead of next week's NFP release. Watch for reactions at the 4H 50-period MA at 45,170.

Levels of interest for Dow Jones Trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Current all-time high: 45,757
  • ATH Resistance Zone: 45,700 (+/- 150 pts)
  • 1.618 Fibonacci extension for potential ATH resistance: 46,260

Support Levels

  • Previous ATH resistance zone, now pivot 45,000 (+/- 150 points)
  • 4H 50-period MA: 45,175
  • 44,400 to 44,500 Main Support

S&P 500: New all-time highs, but lacking conviction

The S&P 500 just reached new all-time highs, with the record standing at 6,496 (CFD, actual index ATH at 6,495). However, the past week of price action is full of wicks, pointing to some lack of conviction around the highs.

Nonetheless, the index is holding very close to its highs; therefore, price action is still far from bearish. The S&P 500 is still out of its higher timeframe upward channel, but bears are still inactive for now, leaving the current trend into play. Price action may stay undecisive until next week!

Levels of interest for S&P 500 Trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Session highs: 6,496 all-time highs
  • All-time high resistance zone: 6,470 to 6,500
  • 6,520 to 6,530 Potential ATH resistance (from Fibonacci extension)

Support Levels

  • End-July Top now Pivot: 6,420 to 6,430
  • 6,400 psychological Support
  • Pre-Powell mini support: 6,350
  • 6,210 to 6,235 Main Support (NFP Lows)

Nasdaq: Bringing back some bullish flows

After unconvincing price action throughout the whole week, Nvidia's earnings seem to have brought some buying flows to the tech-focused index. Look at reactions as the current buying is stepping into the pre-NFP highs resistance zone levels.

Levels to watch for Nasdaq trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Current all-time highs: 23,986
  • 23,500 Support turned resistance
  • 23,732 NFP highs acting as immediate resistance

Support Levels

  • Weekly lows: 23,300
  • 23,000 Key Support
  • 22,700 support at NFP lows
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,229

data.title: Fidelity Select Consumer Discretionary Portfolio Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Market Review

Consumer discretionary stocks gained 11.51% in the second quarter of 2025, according to the MSCI U.S. IMI Consumer Discretionary 25/50 Index, finishing ahead of the 10.94 advance of the S&P 500® index, despite softening consumer sentiment and spending during the three-month period. Meanwhile, the broader U.S. stock market strongly gained this quarter, ending June at an all-time high after erasing a brief but steep early-April decline that followed President Trump's announcement of a sweeping tariff plan.

Against the broad market rally, more cyclical stocks and other areas of the market, including consumer discretionary, generally outperformed the S&P 500, while traditionally defensive sectors lagged. After the pandemic, a historically tight market fueled strong wage growth and a surge in consumption spending that boosted economic growth. These trends moderated in Q2 as federal government funding cuts, layoffs, and ever-changing government policies, especially tariffs, led to a decline in consumer sentiment and slower spending. Meanwhile, interest rates remain persistently high, and although expectations of cuts to the Federal Reserve's rate target persist, the timeline has been pushed out due to concerns regarding potential inflationary impacts from tariffs.

Still, the sector was lifted by the two largest constituents in the sector, electric vehicle maker Tesla (TSLA) (+23%) and e-commerce giant Amazon (AMZN).com (+15%). Both experienced a strong gain, in part because many investors favored mega-cap tech-related stocks this quarter. Leisure industries also performed well, led by the hotels, resorts & cruise lines (+22%) and casinos & gaming (+19%) groups.

Conversely, higher mortgage rates and stretched purchase affordability continued to apply considerable pressure to the housing market and related areas. As a result, the homebuilding and home improvement retail industries each returned roughly -1%. Apparel, accessories & luxury goods (-1%) stocks also fell as many of these retailers faced the headwinds of tariff and economic uncertainty, as well as slowing sales.

Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund advanced 10.92%, modestly underperforming the MSCI U.S. IMI Consumer Discretionary 25/50 Index.

Stock selection and an overweight in the home improvement retail group detracted from the fund's performance versus the MSCI sector index in Q2. This was largely due to our sizable overweight in industry giant Lowe's (LOW) (-4%). Investors reacted negatively to the company's Q1 financial results reported in May, which met revenue expectations and beat earnings. Both revenue and earnings fell year over year, with Lowe's management citing challenges such as pressure on big ticket do-it-yourself home projects, and ongoing housing market challenges and macro uncertainty.

Still, we preferred Lowe's over rival Home Depot (HD) (+1%), a stock that suffered from similar headwinds this quarter. Lowe's was the fund's third-biggest overweight and Home Depot our largest underweight at the end of June. Our large underweight in Home Depot was a key relative contributor this quarter, given the stock's underperformance. At quarter end, Home Depot and Lowe's were the fund's No. 3 and No. 4 largest holdings, respectively.

Another relative detractor was avoiding index component and food delivery service DoorDash (+35%). DoorDash reported strong Q1 earnings and revenue in May, and the company swung to a profit.

Conversely, the fund's top relative contributor this quarter was an out-of-index position in Canadian apparel retailer Aritzia (OTCPK:ATZAF) was helpful. The fund's shares of Aritzia gained about +47% the past three months, as the stock continued to benefit from strong revenue growth and strategic expansion in the U.S.
